skill.name: ai-avatar-video
skill.description: Create AI avatar and talking head videos via inference.sh CLI. Recommended: P-Video-Avatar (fastest, cheapest, built-in TTS). Also: OmniHuman, Fabric, PixVerse. Capabilities: audio-driven avatars, text-to-avatar, lipsync videos, talking head generation, virtual presenters. Use for: AI presenters, explainer videos, virtual influencers, dubbing, marketing videos. Triggers: ai avatar, talking head, lipsync, avatar video, virtual presenter, ai spokesperson, audio driven video, heygen alternative, synthesia alternative, talking avatar, lip sync, video avatar, ai presenter, digital human
skill.summary: Generate AI avatar and talking head videos from images, audio, or text scripts
skill.author: inference-skills

security_audit.summary: Documentation skill for AI video generation via inference.sh CLI. All static findings are false positives. The external_commands (29) are example CLI commands in code blocks demonstrating belt tool usage. The network URLs (20) reference the inference.sh service API endpoints and documentation. The weak_crypto flag (1) is a false positive triggered by YAML frontmatter text mentioning 'algorithm'. No malicious code, command injection, or data exfiltration patterns present.

high_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Flag (False Positive)
high_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 'weak_crypto: Weak cryptographic algorithm' at SKILL.md:3. This is a false positive. Line 3 contains YAML frontmatter describing the skill capabilities. The analyzer likely detected the word 'algorithm' in text like 'talking head generation, virtual presenters'. No cryptographic code exists in this documentation file.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged line is plain YAML frontmatter with description text. No cryptographic functions, MD5 usage, or weak encryption patterns exist in the file.

medium_findings.title: External Commands Documentation (False Positive)
medium_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 29 instances of 'external_commands' (Ruby/shell backtick execution) at various SKILL.md lines. These are all shell command examples displayed in fenced code blocks (```bash blocks). The backtick detection likely triggered on code block syntax. These are documented CLI commands (`belt app run ...`) demonstrating proper belt CLI usage for the inference.sh service. No command injection vulnerabilities exist - the commands are static examples showing API usage patterns.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: All flagged lines contain shell command examples inside fenced code blocks (```bash ... ```). These are read-only documentation examples showing belt CLI usage patterns. No user input handling or command concatenation exists.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs to External Service (False Positive)
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 20 instances of 'hardcoded URLs' (network pattern). These URLs point to: inference.sh service endpoints, documentation links, and image assets. All URLs are legitimate references to the inference.sh service that this skill documents. No suspicious external connections or data exfiltration detected.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All URLs are legitimate references to inference.sh API endpoints, documentation, and image assets. This is expected behavior for a documentation skill describing an external API service.

content.user_title: Create AI Avatar and Talking Head Videos
content.value_statement: Creating professional AI avatar videos traditionally requires complex video editing or expensive SaaS platforms. This skill provides a unified interface to generate talking head videos from images, audio, or text scripts using the inference.sh CLI.
actual_capabilities: Generate talking head videos from portrait images with synchronized lip movements
Create avatar videos from text scripts with built-in text-to-speech (P-Video-Avatar)
Sync existing videos or images to new audio tracks for dubbing
Drive multiple characters in single images with different audio tracks (OmniHuman 1.5)
Generate professional portrait images for avatar creation (via p-image integration)
Produce lip-synced videos in multiple languages for localization workflows
limitations: Requires inference.sh account and CLI authentication
Video generation is asynchronous and may take seconds to minutes depending on model
Output quality depends on input image quality and audio clarity
Some models require separate TTS generation (OmniHuman, PixVerse)

use_cases.title: Product Demo Videos
use_cases.description: Create engaging product demonstrations with an AI presenter. Upload a professional portrait and script your talking points - the avatar delivers your message with natural lip synchronization.

use_cases.title: Training Content Localization
use_cases.description: Translate training videos into multiple languages. Transcribe the original, translate the script, generate new audio, and sync to your presenter avatar for consistent global training materials.

use_cases.title: Social Media Content Creation
use_cases.description: Produce consistent avatar content for social channels. Generate talking head videos from portrait images with AI-generated voices, reducing video production costs and turnaround time.
use_cases.target_user: Content creators and social media managers

prompt_templates.title: Basic Avatar from Script
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate an avatar video using a portrait image with a text script and AI voice
prompt_templates.scenario: When you have a portrait photo and want the person to speak a specific script

prompt_templates.title: Avatar from Audio File
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a talking head video that syncs an existing portrait to a provided audio file
prompt_templates.scenario: When you have pre-recorded audio and want to add a visual avatar

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Language Dubbing
prompt_templates.prompt: Transcribe, translate, and create a lip-synced avatar version of a video in a target language
prompt_templates.scenario: When localizing video content for international audiences

prompt_templates.title: Full Portrait + Avatar Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a portrait image first, then create an avatar video from that portrait with TTS
prompt_templates.scenario: When you do not have a portrait image and need to create both

output_examples.input: Portrait image of a professional + script: 'Welcome to our quarterly review...'
output_examples.output: A video file showing the portrait image with realistic lip movements synchronized to the generated speech audio, delivered as a downloadable video file.

output_examples.input: Portrait image + existing audio file of a speech
output_examples.output: A talking head video where the person in the image appears to deliver the speech with natural facial movements and accurate lip synchronization.

output_examples.input: Original training video + translated Spanish audio
output_examples.output: A version of the training video with the original visual presenter now speaking the translated Spanish audio with proper lip sync.

best_practices: Use high-quality, front-facing portrait photos with clear visibility of the face and good lighting for best results
Generate audio with clear speech and minimal background noise before creating avatar videos
Use P-Video-Avatar for the best balance of speed, cost, and quality - it includes built-in TTS and 1080p output
anti_patterns: Do not use low-quality or heavily filtered portrait images - avatar lip sync quality depends on input image clarity
Do not use audio with significant background noise - this degrades lip sync accuracy
Do not skip the audio generation step when using models without built-in TTS (OmniHuman, PixVerse)

faq.question: What is the recommended model for avatar video generation?
faq.answer: P-Video-Avatar is recommended. It is 18x faster and 6x cheaper than alternatives, supports built-in TTS with 30 voices in 10 languages, and outputs at 1080p resolution.

faq.question: How do I create an avatar if I do not have a portrait image?
faq.answer: Generate a portrait image first using the p-image model (pruna/p-image) with prompts like 'professional headshot portrait of a young woman, neutral background', then use that image as input for avatar creation.

faq.question: Can I use my own voice instead of AI-generated speech?
faq.answer: Yes. Upload your own audio file using the 'audio' parameter instead of 'voice_script'. This is supported by all models including P-Video-Avatar.

faq.question: How do I localize videos into other languages?
faq.answer: Use fast-whisper to transcribe the original video, translate the text, generate new speech with kokoro-tts in the target language, then sync using latentsync-1-6.

faq.question: What image formats are supported?
faq.answer: Provide image URLs (http:// or https://). Supported formats include JPG, PNG, and WebP. For best results, use high-quality portraits with front-facing composition.

faq.question: How long does video generation take?
faq.answer: Generation time varies by model. P-Video-Avatar processes at ~1.83 seconds per second of video. OmniHuman 1.5 takes ~28s per second. Higher resolutions take longer to process.
